source_paper_title: On the Impossibility of Convergence of Mixed Strategies with Optimal No-Regret Learning
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Muthukumar_2025, title={On the Impossibility of Convergence of Mixed Strategies with Optimal No-Regret Learning}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0016}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0016}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Muthukumar, Vidya and Phade, Soham and Sahai, Anant}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={2794–2833} }
context:
Section 3.5 ("Beyond the monotonicity assumption: A conjecture"), page 24-25; Conjecture 3.12. Also reiterated as a main future-work question in Section 5 (Conclusion), page 29.

We conjecture below that last-iterate oscillations will continue to hold for mean-based strategies that are not monotonic.

Conjecture 3.12. If both players 1 and 2 use mean-based (not necessarily monotonic) repeated game strategies \(\{f_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) and \(\{g_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) that are uniformly no-regret and each have a regret rate of \((1/2,c)\), then the pair of mixed strategies \((P_t,Q_t)\) does not converge almost surely.
title: Prove divergence of last-iterate mixed strategies without monotonicity in 2x2 games
background_latex:
Consider an infinitely repeated two-player \(2\times 2\) stage game with action sets \(\{0,1\}\) for each player and payoff matrices \(G,H\in\mathbb{R}^{2\times 2}\). At each round \(t=1,2,\dots\), player 1 chooses a mixed action \(P_t\in[0,1]\) (probability of playing action 1) and then realizes a pure action \(I_t\in\{0,1\}\) with \(\mathbb{P}(I_t=1\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})=P_t\); similarly player 2 chooses \(Q_t\in[0,1]\) and realizes \(J_t\in\{0,1\}\) with \(\mathbb{P}(J_t=1\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})=Q_t\). Players observe only realized actions (not the opponent's mixtures), so the history up to time \(t-1\) is \(\mathcal{F}_{t-1}:=\sigma\big((I_s,J_s)_{s=1}^{t-1}\big)\).

Define empirical averages of realized actions by
\[\widehat P_t:=\frac1t\sum_{s=1}^t I_s,\qquad \widehat Q_t:=\frac1t\sum_{s=1}^t J_s.\]
A (self-agnostic) repeated-game strategy for player 1 is a sequence of measurable maps \(f_t:\{0,1\}^{t-1}\to[0,1]\) so that \(P_t=f_t((J_s)_{s=1}^{t-1})\); analogously player 2 uses maps \(g_t\) with \(Q_t=g_t((I_s)_{s=1}^{t-1})\). A strategy is mean-based if there exist functions (still denoted) \(f_t:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) such that \(P_t=f_t(\widehat Q_{t-1})\), and similarly \(Q_t=g_t(\widehat P_{t-1})\).

Player 1's (external) regret up to time \(T\) against an opponent realization sequence \((J_t)_{t=1}^T\) is
\[\operatorname{Reg}_1(T):=\max_{i\in\{0,1\}}\sum_{t=1}^T G(i,J_t)-\sum_{t=1}^T G(I_t,J_t).\]
A strategy is uniformly no-regret with rate \((r,c)\) if for all \(T\) and all opponent action sequences, \(\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Reg}_1(T)]\le c\,T^{r}\), where expectation is over the player’s internal randomness (and any randomness in the opponent strategy), and the optimal rate is \(r=1/2\).

Assume the stage game is competitive in the sense that it has a unique completely mixed Nash equilibrium \((p^*,q^*)\in(0,1)^2\).
problem_definition_latex:
Assume both players use mean-based repeated-game strategies \(\{f_t\}_{t\ge1}\) and \(\{g_t\}_{t\ge1}\) (not assumed monotone) such that each strategy is uniformly no-regret with regret rate \((1/2,c)\) for some finite \(c\). Determine whether it must hold that the last-iterate mixed-strategy process \((P_t,Q_t)\in[0,1]^2\) fails to converge almost surely; i.e., prove or refute that
\[\mathbb{P}\big(\lim_{t\to\infty}(P_t,Q_t) \text{ exists}\big)=0\]
or equivalently that \((P_t,Q_t)\) does not converge almost surely (in particular, it cannot converge almost surely to the Nash equilibrium \((p^*,q^*)\)).
latex:

Player 1's (external) regret up to time \(T\) against an opponent realization sequence \((J_t)_{t=1}^T\) is

location_in_paper: Section 3.5 ("Beyond the monotonicity assumption: A conjecture"), page 24-25; Conjecture 3.12. Also reiterated as a main future-work question in Section 5 (Conclusion), page 29.
open_problem_quote:
We conjecture below that last-iterate oscillations will continue to hold for mean-based strategies that are not monotonic.

Conjecture 3.12. If both players 1 and 2 use mean-based (not necessarily monotonic) repeated game strategies \(\{f_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) and \(\{g_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) that are uniformly no-regret and each have a regret rate of \((1/2,c)\), then the pair of mixed strategies \((P_t,Q_t)\) does not converge almost surely.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample would require explicitly constructing non-monotone mean-based no-regret update maps \(f_t,g_t\) and then testing almost-sure convergence, which is hard to certify computationally. However, one can search over parameterized non-monotone families and use long-run simulation plus statistical diagnostics as a proxy to find plausible convergent dynamics.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate strategies can be represented as code for maps \(f_t,g_t\) (e.g., piecewise functions or neural nets taking \(\widehat Q_{t-1}\) as input) with a computable fitness signal combining empirical regret and a convergence metric for \((P_t,Q_t)\). The main limitation is that convergence is asymptotic and noisy to estimate from finite simulations, so the fitness proxy may be imperfect and expensive.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Muthukumar–Phade–Sahai, 2022) proves that in competitive 2×2 games with a unique completely mixed Nash equilibrium \((p^*,q^*)\), last-iterate mixed strategies cannot converge almost surely when both players use strategies that are (i) uniformly no-regret with optimal \(O(\sqrt{T})\) rate, (ii) mean-based (depend only on the opponent’s empirical action frequency), and (iii) monotone in that empirical frequency. The core mechanism is a “fluctuation sensitivity” property: any optimal-rate no-regret strategy must react by a constant amount to \(\Theta(t^{-1/2})\) deviations in the opponent’s empirical frequency, and such deviations occur infinitely often due to sampling noise even if the opponent plays the equilibrium mixture i.i.d. This yields persistent oscillations of the last iterate.

The open problem asks whether the monotonicity assumption can be removed while retaining the same conclusion (almost-sure nonconvergence of \((P_t,Q_t)\)). Among the forward citations provided, the closest partial progress is work on last-iterate convergence under uncoupled bandit feedback in zero-sum games, which establishes sharp lower bounds on achievable last-iterate convergence rates and provides matching algorithms. While it does not prove almost-sure divergence for optimal \(\sqrt{T}\)-regret mean-based dynamics, it reinforces the broader message that last-iterate behavior is intrinsically difficult under realization-only feedback. A separate line of work on higher-order uncoupled dynamics shows that local stabilization of completely mixed Nash equilibria is possible in continuous time with auxiliary states, indicating that convergence can be achieved in richer dynamical models, but this does not directly translate to the discrete-time, optimal no-regret, mean-based setting.

Overall, based on the limited forward-citation set provided, there is no definitive resolution of the monotonicity-free conjecture. The main gap remains to either (a) extend the fluctuation-sensitivity/oscillation argument to arbitrary (possibly nonmonotone) mean-based mappings \(f_t,g_t\) with optimal regret, or (b) construct a counterexample: a pair of nonmonotone mean-based optimal no-regret strategies whose last iterates converge almost surely in some competitive 2×2 game. Promising directions include identifying weaker structural properties implied by optimal regret (e.g., sign changes or bounded variation constraints on \(f_t\)) that still force oscillations under \(\Theta(t^{-1/2})\) empirical fluctuations, or leveraging stabilization ideas that require additional state beyond the empirical mean, thereby clarifying whether mean-basedness itself (rather than monotonicity) is the true obstruction.

sources.title: The harder path: Last iterate convergence for uncoupled learning in zero-sum games with bandit feedback
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves lower bounds and gives matching algorithms for last-iterate convergence in uncoupled two-player zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback. In particular, under the assumption that the opponent’s sampled action is never observed, for any p∈(0,2] no learning sequence can achieve L^p last-iterate convergence rate better than order t^{-1/(2+p)} uniformly over a family of 2×2 Bernoulli games M_ε (Theorem 5.1), and for p>2 it implies no better than order t^{-1/4} (Corollary 5.3). It also provides uncoupled algorithms achieving these rates up to logs: Algorithm 1 achieves L^p rate \(\tilde O(t^{-1/(2+p)})\) (Theorem 6.2), and a regularized mirror-descent/EXP3 variant achieves \(\tilde O(t^{-1/4})\) in L^2 with a doubling-trick meta-procedure (Theorem 8.1).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether, in competitive 2×2 games with a unique completely mixed Nash equilibrium, any pair of mean-based strategies with optimal \(\sqrt T\)-regret (rate (1/2,c)) must have almost-sure divergence of the last-iterate mixed strategies (without assuming monotonicity). This paper does not address that question directly: it studies zero-sum games with bandit feedback and focuses on achievable rates of last-iterate convergence in expectation/L^p of exploitability gap, not almost-sure convergence/divergence of the actual mixed strategies \((P_t,Q_t)\) under optimal no-regret learning.

However, it is partially relevant because it (i) explicitly constructs simple 2×2 games where last-iterate convergence is information-theoretically hard under uncoupled/bandit feedback, and (ii) discusses impossibility of almost-sure convergence for some no-regret algorithms (citing Muthukumar et al. 2020). Its lower-bound construction relies on the opponent’s mixed strategy approaching a minimax policy that makes the other player’s actions nearly indistinguishable, which is conceptually related to mechanisms that can prevent stable last-iterate behavior.

Still, it neither proves that last-iterate mixed strategies must diverge a.s. under optimal no-regret mean-based dynamics, nor provides a counterexample of convergent last-iterates with optimal regret in the general competitive 2×2 setting. In fact, it provides algorithms that do converge in last iterate (in L^p exploitability) but at slower rates than \(T^{-1/2}\), so they are not ‘optimal no-regret’ in the sense of the open problem.

sources.title: Higher-Order Uncoupled Learning Dynamics and Nash Equilibrium
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves local exponential convergence to an isolated completely mixed Nash equilibrium in general finite games using higher-order uncoupled continuous-time dynamics. In particular, for any finite game with an isolated completely mixed-strategy NE \(x^*\) satisfying a nonsingularity/regularity condition (Assumption 2.1), there exist higher-order replicator dynamics of the form (18) (uncoupled, with auxiliary states and a washout filter) such that \((x^*,0,0)\) is locally exponentially stable (Theorem 4.1). More generally, for any standard-order uncoupled dynamics satisfying certain local nonsingularity conditions, there exists a higher-order uncoupled extension making \((x^*,0,0)\) locally exponentially stable (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether, in repeated \(2\times2\) competitive games with bandit feedback and mean-based (history-through-empirical-mean) strategies achieving optimal \(O(\sqrt{T})\) regret, the last-iterate mixed strategies \((P_t,Q_t)\) must diverge a.s. without assuming monotonicity. This paper studies a different model: continuous-time payoff-based (vector-payoff) uncoupled learning dynamics with auxiliary states, focusing on local stabilization of completely mixed NE. It provides positive convergence constructions (local exponential stability) rather than divergence/impossibility, and it does not analyze no-regret guarantees, discrete-time last-iterate behavior, or mean-based bandit feedback. Thus it neither proves nor refutes the almost-sure divergence claim in the open problem. At most, it offers control-theoretic stabilization techniques suggesting that convergence can be engineered in richer-information or higher-order settings, but these do not directly apply to the mean-based optimal no-regret bandit setting of the open problem.
